children.name: Malaconotus
children.description: The large bushshrikes — a genus of robust, heavy-billed African passerines with powerful hooked bills adapted for taking large prey including vertebrates. They are the largest bushshrikes, with a deliberate, slow-moving foraging style that belies their fierce predatory nature. Their calls are deep, mournful whistles that carry long distances through the forest.

children.name: Malaconotus blanchoti
children.description: A large, powerfully built bushshrike with a grey head, olive-green back, and bright yellow underparts, widespread across sub-Saharan Africa. It hunts from a concealed perch, taking large insects, lizards, snakes, and small birds with its massive hooked bill. Its far-carrying, melancholy whistle gives it the local name 'ghost bird' in parts of its range.

children.name: Malaconotus cruentus
children.description: A large, striking bushshrike with a grey head, olive-green upperparts, and brilliant orange-red underparts, found in lowland forests of West and Central Africa. It is a powerful predator that takes large insects, small birds, and reptiles. Its deep, whistled calls are far-carrying and melancholy.

children.name: Malaconotus monteiri
children.description: A large bushshrike with a grey head, green back, and bright yellow underparts, found in the highlands of Angola and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. It inhabits montane forests and bamboo thickets at elevations above 1,500 metres. Its deep, whistled song is a characteristic sound of the Angolan highlands.

children.name: Malaconotus lagdeni
children.description: A large, striking bushshrike with a grey head, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts with a distinctive orange wash on the breast, found in montane forests of eastern Africa from Uganda to Mozambique. It is a shy and elusive species, more often heard than seen. Its deep, mournful whistles echo through the mountain forests.

children.name: Malaconotus gladiator
children.description: A large, robust bushshrike with a grey head, green breast, and bright yellow belly, restricted to the montane forests of the Albertine Rift in eastern Africa. It is a little-known species that inhabits the canopy of high-altitude forests. Its powerful, hooked bill is used to capture large insects and small vertebrates.

children.name: Chlorophoneus
children.description: The green-and-yellow bushshrikes — a genus of medium-sized, colourful bushshrikes with greenish upperparts and bright yellow or orange underparts. They are more arboreal and slender-billed than Malaconotus, feeding primarily on insects gleaned from foliage. Their songs are rich, melodious, and often repetitive.

children.name: Chlorophoneus sulfureopectus
children.description: A beautiful bushshrike with olive-green upperparts, a grey head, and a bright yellow-orange breast, widespread across sub-Saharan African woodlands and savannas. It forages methodically in the canopy, gleaning caterpillars and other insects from leaves. Its loud, musical, whistled song is a characteristic sound of African woodlands.

children.name: Chlorophoneus multicolor
children.description: A brilliantly coloured bushshrike with green upperparts, a red throat, and a yellow belly crossed by a black breast band, found in lowland forests of West and Central Africa. It forages in the canopy, gleaning insects from foliage. Its rich, whistled song is a series of clear, descending notes.

children.name: Chlorophoneus nigrifrons
children.description: A striking bushshrike with green upperparts, a black forehead and face, and bright orange-yellow underparts, found in montane and lowland forests of eastern and central Africa. It is an active, acrobatic forager in the forest canopy, often hanging upside-down to glean insects. Its song is a series of clear, fluty whistles.

children.name: Chlorophoneus kupeensis
children.description: A critically endangered bushshrike restricted to the slopes of Mount Kupe in western Cameroon, with green upperparts, a grey head, and yellow underparts. It was discovered only in 1951 and remains one of Africa's rarest and most threatened passerines. Its population is estimated at fewer than 1,000 individuals.

children.name: Dryoscopus
children.description: The puffbacks — a genus of medium-sized bushshrikes named for the male's dramatic display in which the loose feathers of the lower back and rump are fluffed out into a striking white ball. They are active, restless foragers with strong, slightly hooked bills, found across sub-Saharan Africa in woodlands and savannas. They are vocal birds with harsh, repetitive calls.

children.name: Dryoscopus cubla
children.description: A striking black-and-white bushshrike in which the male puffs out his snow-white rump feathers in a spectacular courtship display, common in woodlands across eastern and southern Africa. It forages actively in the canopy, gleaning insects from leaves and branches. Its loud, grating call is often delivered as a duet between paired birds.

children.name: Tchagra
children.description: The tchagras — a genus of medium-sized bushshrikes with a distinctive crown pattern featuring a black lateral stripe edged with white, giving them a capped appearance. They have relatively slender, hooked bills and long, graduated tails, and are more terrestrial than other bushshrikes. They inhabit scrub, savanna, and forest edge habitats across Africa.

children.name: Tchagra australis
children.description: A medium-sized bushshrike with a brown crown, a bold black eye-stripe, and grey-brown upperparts, widespread across sub-Saharan African woodlands and savannas. It forages close to the ground, hopping through low scrub and dropping onto prey from low perches. Its accelerating, bubbling song is a characteristic sound of African bushveld.

children.name: Rhodophoneus
children.description: A monotypic genus of bushshrike containing the Rosy-patched Bushshrike, a striking inhabitant of arid scrublands in eastern Africa. It has a unique plumage pattern with a vivid crimson patch on the throat and breast, and is more terrestrial than other bushshrikes, often running on the ground.

children.name: Rhodophoneus cruentus
children.description: A distinctive bushshrike of arid and semi-arid scrublands in eastern Africa, with grey-brown upperparts, white underparts, and a brilliant crimson patch on the throat and upper breast. It is more terrestrial than most bushshrikes, running rapidly on the ground in search of insects. Its song is a series of clear, descending whistles.

children.name: Telophorus
children.description: A genus of beautiful, colourful bushshrikes with green upperparts and brightly coloured underparts in shades of orange, red, or yellow. They are forest and woodland dwellers that forage actively in the canopy, gleaning insects from leaves. Their songs are rich, musical, and often delivered in duet.

children.name: Telophorus viridis
children.description: A brilliantly coloured bushshrike with green upperparts, a yellow throat, a black breast band, and a bright red belly, found in forests and woodlands of East and southern Africa. It forages actively in the canopy, gleaning insects from foliage. Its rich, whistled song is a series of clear, bell-like notes.

children.name: Telophorus dohertyi
children.description: A stunning bushshrike with green upperparts, a bright red throat and breast, and a yellow belly, restricted to the montane forests of the Albertine Rift in central Africa. It is a shy, elusive species of dense undergrowth, more often heard than seen. Its song is a series of rich, melodious whistles.

children.name: Telophorus sulfureopectus
children.description: A beautiful bushshrike with green upperparts, a grey head, and a bright yellow breast, widespread in woodlands and savannas across sub-Saharan Africa. It forages deliberately in the canopy, gleaning caterpillars and other insects from leaves. Its loud, whistled song is one of the most characteristic sounds of African woodland.

children.name: Laniarius
children.description: The boubous and gonoleks — a genus of colourful bushshrikes with strong, stout bills and a characteristic duetting behaviour in which pairs sing precisely coordinated antiphonals. They are among the most brightly coloured bushshrikes, often combining black, white, and brilliant crimson or yellow underparts. They are secretive but highly vocal, inhabiting dense thickets and riparian vegetation.

children.name: Laniarius atrococcineus
children.description: A breathtaking bushshrike with jet-black upperparts and a brilliant crimson breast and belly, found in the arid savannas of southern Africa. The male and female are identical in plumage, and pairs stay together year-round, defending their territory with loud, synchronised duets. Despite its brilliant colours, it can be surprisingly difficult to spot as it skulks in dense thorn scrub.
